title: ICU Liberation Using the ABCDEF Bundle in a Mechanically Ventilated 60-Year-Old (Day 3 of Intubation for Severe Pneumonia): Analgesia-First/Light Sedation Targeted to RASS, Daily Paired Spontaneous Awakening + Breathing Trials, CAM-ICU Delirium Monitoring, Early Mobility, and Family Engagement to Prevent Oversedation, Delirium, ICU-Acquired Weakness, and Post-Intensive Care Syndrome
subtitle: ICU LIBERATION is a coordinated, evidence-based approach to FREE critically ill patients from the preventable HARMS of prolonged intensive care — oversedation, DELIRIUM, ICU-ACQUIRED WEAKNESS, prolonged mechanical ventilation, and the long-term physical/cognitive/psychological impairment of POST-INTENSIVE CARE SYNDROME (PICS) — operationalized as the SCCM 'ABCDEF' BUNDLE, implemented daily by a coordinated interdisciplinary team. A — ASSESS, PREVENT, AND MANAGE PAIN: assess pain routinely with a VALIDATED scale (a numeric/self-report scale if the patient can communicate; the CPOT or BPS behavioral scales for patients unable to self-report) and treat pain FIRST ('analgesia-first'/analgosedation), because untreated pain drives agitation that is often mistakenly treated with MORE sedative. B — BOTH spontaneous awakening trials (SAT) and spontaneous breathing trials (SBT): perform a DAILY SAT (pause/lighten sedation, after a safety screen, to let the patient wake and be assessed) COORDINATED with a DAILY SBT (assess readiness to breathe with minimal/no support → toward extubation); PAIRED SAT+SBT shortens ventilator days, ICU length of stay, and improves survival; monitor during the trials and, if a trial fails, resume sedation at the LOWEST effective dose and retry the next day. C — CHOICE OF ANALGESIA AND SEDATION: target LIGHT sedation (commonly RASS 0 to -2 — calm and arousable, NOT deeply sedated) using a validated sedation scale (RASS or SAS), titrate to the lightest effective level, and PREFER NON-BENZODIAZEPINE sedatives (propofol, dexmedetomidine) while AVOIDING BENZODIAZEPINES, which are DELIRIOGENIC and prolong ventilation; avoid oversedation (deep sedation worsens outcomes). D — DELIRIUM, ASSESS/PREVENT/MANAGE: routinely SCREEN for delirium with a validated tool (CAM-ICU or ICDSC) every shift; PREVENT and treat delirium by addressing modifiable risks NON-pharmacologically first — minimize sedatives/benzodiazepines, treat pain, REORIENT, provide sensory aids (glasses/hearing aids), promote SLEEP (day-night cues, reduce noise/light, cluster care), and mobilize; routine ANTIPSYCHOTICS are NOT recommended for delirium prevention (and have limited role in treatment) — the mainstay is removing precipitants; delirium independently predicts higher mortality and long-term cognitive impairment. E — EARLY MOBILITY AND EXERCISE: progressively mobilize the patient as soon as safe (passive/active range of motion → sitting at the edge of bed → standing → ambulating), EVEN WHILE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED when hemodynamically/respiratorily stable, with PT/OT; early mobility reduces ICU-acquired weakness, delirium, and ventilator/ICU days. F — FAMILY ENGAGEMENT AND EMPOWERMENT: actively involve the family — presence/open visitation, participation in interdisciplinary ROUNDS and care, communication, and shared decision-making — which supports the patient, reduces delirium, eases PICS-Family, and aligns care with the patient's values. The bundle elements are SYNERGISTIC and reinforce one another (lighter sedation enables awakening/breathing trials, delirium screening, and mobility; family presence aids reorientation), and they require DAILY GOAL-SETTING and COORDINATION among the nurse, intensivist, respiratory therapist, PT/OT, pharmacist, and family on rounds — making this fundamentally a MANAGEMENT-OF-CARE / care-coordination competency. ADDITIONAL preventive ICU care woven through the bundle: SLEEP promotion + a normal day-night cycle; ventilator-associated event/pneumonia prevention (head-of-bed elevation, oral care, daily assessment of extubation readiness); pressure-injury and VTE prophylaxis; nutrition; and humane, comfort-focused care of an awake, often anxious ventilated patient (communication methods, reassurance). NURSING ROLE: assess and treat pain first with validated tools; target and titrate LIGHT sedation (RASS-guided, non-benzodiazepine), avoiding oversedation; perform daily paired SAT/SBT with appropriate safety screening and monitoring; screen for delirium each shift (CAM-ICU) and prevent it by removing precipitants (minimize sedatives, reorient, sleep, mobilize, sensory aids, family); progress early mobility with the team; engage and empower the family; and coordinate the interdisciplinary daily plan — all to liberate the patient sooner and prevent delirium, weakness, prolonged ventilation, and PICS

patient.name: R. Delgado
patient.code: Full code; intubated/sedated; wife is healthcare proxy + engaged; goal = liberate from sedation and the ventilator as soon as safe while preventing delirium, weakness, and post-intensive care syndrome
patient.admitDate: ICU day 3, mechanically ventilated for severe community-acquired pneumonia with hypoxemic respiratory failure (improving — FiO2/PEEP weaning, infection responding to antibiotics). He is on a continuous sedation infusion; the question for today is how to manage analgesia/sedation, assess readiness to wean, monitor for delirium, and begin mobility — i.e., to implement the ABCDEF (ICU Liberation) bundle rather than keep him deeply sedated. His wife is at the bedside and wants to help.
patient.room: ICU, mechanically ventilated; continuous monitoring; interdisciplinary team (intensivist, ICU nurse, respiratory therapist, PT/OT, pharmacist) with daily goal-setting rounds; family engaged

vitals.temp: 37.2°C; mechanically ventilated, currently DEEPLY sedated (RASS -4) on a sedation infusion; pain assessed with CPOT (unable to self-report while sedated); the team identifies that he is OVER-sedated relative to a light-sedation goal and plans analgesia-first, lighter sedation, and a spontaneous awakening trial

vitals.time: 0800 — A + C: pain-first + lighten sedation to RASS goal
vitals.temp: 37.1°C; pain treated first (analgesia optimized); benzodiazepine sedation switched/avoided in favor of a non-benzodiazepine agent (e.g., dexmedetomidine/propofol) titrated to a LIGHT-sedation target of RASS 0 to -2 (calm, arousable); avoiding deep sedation; CPOT used to ensure pain is controlled as sedation lightens

vitals.time: 0830 — B: paired spontaneous awakening + breathing trial
vitals.hr: 94 (briefly up as he awakens, then settles)
vitals.spo2: 96% on a spontaneous breathing trial (minimal support)
vitals.temp: 37.1°C; after a safety screen, a SPONTANEOUS AWAKENING TRIAL (sedation paused/lightened) is coordinated with a SPONTANEOUS BREATHING TRIAL; he awakens, follows commands, and tolerates the SBT (stable vitals, adequate spontaneous breathing) → assessed as ready for extubation; if he had failed, sedation would resume at the LOWEST effective dose and retry tomorrow

vitals.time: 1000 — D + E: delirium screen + early mobility (post-extubation)
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; extubated after the successful SAT/SBT; CAM-ICU screening performed (negative for delirium); delirium prevention in place (reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, sleep promotion, minimal sedatives); EARLY MOBILITY begun with PT/OT (dangling/sitting at the edge of the bed, progressing as tolerated); family at bedside assisting with reorientation

36.9°C; light sedation no longer needed; CAM-ICU remains negative; progressing mobility (standing/ambulating with assistance); sleeping at night with day-night cues + clustered care; family engaged in rounds; no ventilator-associated event; on track for ICU step-down — avoiding oversedation, delirium, weakness, and prolonged ventilation

—; teaching contrast — had the team kept him DEEPLY sedated on a benzodiazepine infusion without daily SAT/SBT, delirium screening, or mobility, the predictable course is more delirium, ICU-acquired weakness, prolonged ventilation, longer ICU stay, and a higher risk of post-intensive care syndrome (PICS) — the harms the ABCDEF bundle prevents

labs.v: 60 y/o man R. Delgado, ICU day 3, mechanically ventilated for severe community-acquired pneumonia with hypoxemic respiratory failure that is now IMPROVING (FiO2 and PEEP weaning, infection responding to antibiotics). He is on a continuous sedation infusion and is currently DEEPLY sedated. The clinical decision today is NOT simply to continue deep sedation, but to implement ICU LIBERATION via the ABCDEF BUNDLE — because prolonged deep sedation and immobility cause PREVENTABLE HARM: oversedation, DELIRIUM, ICU-ACQUIRED WEAKNESS, prolonged mechanical ventilation and ICU stay, and long-term POST-INTENSIVE CARE SYNDROME (PICS — persistent physical, cognitive, and psychological impairment after critical illness, which also affects families/'PICS-F'). The modern evidence is clear that LIGHTER sedation, daily awakening and breathing trials, delirium monitoring/prevention, early mobility, and family engagement IMPROVE outcomes (fewer ventilator days, less delirium, shorter ICU stays, better survival and long-term function). PMH: pneumonia/respiratory failure (improving); no contraindication to mobilization noted (hemodynamically stable, oxygenation improving). SOCIAL: married; wife is the healthcare proxy, present and eager to participate; the patient was previously independent. The nurse, as part of a coordinated interdisciplinary team (intensivist, respiratory therapist, PT/OT, pharmacist, and family), implements the bundle with DAILY GOAL-SETTING — a quintessential MANAGEMENT-OF-CARE / care-coordination responsibility. The aim across this shift: control pain, lighten sedation to a defined target, assess readiness to liberate from the ventilator, monitor for and prevent delirium, begin mobility, and engage the family — liberating the patient sooner and preventing the harms of prolonged ICU care.
labs.r: ICU Liberation (the ABCDEF bundle) prevents the harms of prolonged ICU care — oversedation, delirium, ICU-acquired weakness, prolonged ventilation, and PICS — by lightening sedation, daily SAT/SBT, delirium monitoring/prevention, early mobility, and family engagement, coordinated by the interdisciplinary team. RN role: implement the bundle with daily goals + coordination (not default to deep sedation).

labs.n: A + C: assess/treat pain first; light, non-benzodiazepine sedation to a RASS target
labs.v: A — ASSESS, PREVENT, AND MANAGE PAIN (do this FIRST): pain is assessed routinely with a VALIDATED tool — a self-report numeric scale if the patient can communicate, or a behavioral scale (CPOT — Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool, or BPS — Behavioral Pain Scale) when the patient cannot self-report (intubated/sedated). Treat pain FIRST ('analgesia-first' or analgosedation), because untreated pain produces agitation, tachycardia, and ventilator dyssynchrony that are frequently MISATTRIBUTED to inadequate sedation and 'treated' with escalating sedatives — worsening oversedation. Use multimodal analgesia (opioids titrated to a pain goal, plus non-opioid adjuncts as appropriate). C — CHOICE OF ANALGESIA AND SEDATION: target LIGHT sedation — commonly RASS (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale) 0 to -2 (calm and cooperative, or lightly sedated but easily AROUSABLE to voice) — assessed with a validated sedation scale (RASS or SAS) every few hours and titrated to the LIGHTEST EFFECTIVE level; DEEP sedation (e.g., RASS -4/-5) is AVOIDED unless specifically indicated (e.g., severe ARDS requiring paralysis, status epilepticus, refractory intracranial hypertension) because it prolongs ventilation and increases delirium. PREFER NON-BENZODIAZEPINE sedatives — PROPOFOL or DEXMEDETOMIDINE — and AVOID BENZODIAZEPINE infusions (e.g., midazolam/lorazepam), which are DELIRIOGENIC and associated with more delirium and longer ventilation (benzodiazepines are reserved for specific indications such as alcohol/sedative withdrawal or seizures). Titrate sedation to the defined RASS goal rather than to deep unconsciousness, and reassess frequently. THIS PATIENT was over-sedated (RASS -4); the team treated pain first (CPOT-guided), switched away from a benzodiazepine to a non-benzodiazepine agent, and titrated to a light-sedation target of RASS 0 to -2 — calm and arousable — setting the stage for the awakening/breathing trial and delirium monitoring.
labs.r: A: assess pain with a VALIDATED tool (self-report, or CPOT/BPS if unable) and treat pain FIRST (untreated pain is mistaken for needing more sedation). C: target LIGHT sedation RASS 0 to -2 (calm/arousable) via a validated scale, titrate to the lightest effective level, AVOID deep sedation and AVOID benzodiazepine infusions (deliriogenic) — prefer propofol/dexmedetomidine. RN role: analgesia-first + light, non-benzodiazepine, RASS-targeted sedation.

labs.n: B: paired daily spontaneous awakening + breathing trials
labs.v: B — BOTH SPONTANEOUS AWAKENING TRIALS (SAT) AND SPONTANEOUS BREATHING TRIALS (SBT), performed DAILY and COORDINATED ('wake up and breathe'): the SAT is a daily interruption/lightening of sedation to allow the patient to awaken and be assessed, and the SBT is a daily assessment of the patient's ability to breathe with minimal or no ventilator support (e.g., a brief trial on pressure support or a T-piece). PAIRING the SAT with the SBT (awaken first, then test breathing) is supported by strong evidence to REDUCE ventilator days, ICU length of stay, and MORTALITY compared with usual care. PROCESS: (1) perform a SAFETY SCREEN before each trial — e.g., not actively requiring escalating sedation for agitation/seizures, no active myocardial ischemia, no need for ongoing neuromuscular blockade, acceptable oxygenation/hemodynamics, no rising intracranial pressure; (2) if the SAFETY SCREEN passes, conduct the SAT (hold/lighten sedation) and observe — a successful SAT is the patient awakening and remaining calm/able to follow commands without severe agitation, dangerous vital-sign changes, respiratory distress, or other failure criteria; (3) if the SAT is passed, proceed to the SBT and assess readiness to wean/extubate (adequate spontaneous breathing, gas exchange, stable vitals, manageable secretions, intact airway protection); (4) if a trial FAILS at any point, STOP and RESUME sedation at the LOWEST dose needed (typically half the prior rate) and RETRY the next day — a failure is information, not a setback. The NURSE and RESPIRATORY THERAPIST coordinate the timing of the SAT and SBT, monitor the patient continuously during the trials, and communicate results to the team for extubation decisions. THIS PATIENT passed the safety screen, had a coordinated SAT (awakened, followed commands) and SBT (tolerated minimal support with stable gas exchange/vitals), and was assessed ready for — and successfully — extubated; had he failed, sedation would have resumed at the lowest effective dose with a retry the next day.
labs.r: B: daily PAIRED Spontaneous Awakening Trial (lighten/hold sedation) + Spontaneous Breathing Trial ('wake up and breathe') after a SAFETY SCREEN → reduces ventilator days, ICU LOS, and mortality; assess readiness to extubate; if a trial fails, resume sedation at the LOWEST effective dose and retry tomorrow. RN + RT coordinate + monitor. RN role: safety-screen, conduct/monitor SAT+SBT, communicate readiness.

labs.v: D — DELIRIUM: ASSESS, PREVENT, AND MANAGE. Delirium (acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention/awareness/cognition) is COMMON in ventilated ICU patients, frequently HYPOACTIVE (quietly inattentive — easily missed without screening), and independently predicts higher mortality, prolonged ventilation/ICU stay, and long-term COGNITIVE impairment. SCREEN routinely (at least every shift and with changes) using a VALIDATED tool — the CAM-ICU (Confusion Assessment Method for the ICU) or the ICDSC (Intensive Care Delirium Screening Checklist) — which can be applied even in nonverbal/ventilated patients. PREVENTION is primarily NON-PHARMACOLOGIC and overlaps the whole bundle: MINIMIZE deliriogenic sedatives (especially BENZODIAZEPINES) and oversedation, TREAT PAIN, REORIENT frequently (clock, calendar, explain care), provide SENSORY aids (glasses, hearing aids), promote SLEEP and a normal day-night cycle (reduce nighttime noise/light, cluster care, minimize unnecessary interruptions), MOBILIZE early, and ENGAGE FAMILY for reorientation/comfort. Importantly, ROUTINE ANTIPSYCHOTICS are NOT recommended to PREVENT delirium and have a LIMITED role in treating it (evidence does not support improved outcomes); the mainstay is identifying and REMOVING PRECIPITANTS (sedatives, untreated pain, infection, metabolic derangements, hypoxia, immobility, sleep deprivation, restraints) — antipsychotics are reserved for severe agitation/distress posing a safety risk, at the lowest dose for the shortest time. THIS PATIENT was screened with CAM-ICU (negative) and had delirium-prevention measures in place (reorientation, sensory aids, sleep promotion, minimized sedatives, early mobility, family) — the lighter sedation and avoidance of benzodiazepines directly reduce his delirium risk.
labs.r: D: screen for delirium every shift with a VALIDATED tool (CAM-ICU/ICDSC) — it's common, often hypoactive/missed, and predicts worse outcomes. PREVENT non-pharmacologically by removing precipitants: minimize sedatives/benzodiazepines + treat pain + reorient + sensory aids + sleep/day-night cues + early mobility + family. Routine ANTIPSYCHOTICS are NOT for prevention (limited treatment role; reserve for dangerous agitation). RN role: CAM-ICU screening + precipitant removal + nonpharmacologic prevention.

labs.n: E + F: early mobility + family engagement; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: E — EARLY MOBILITY AND EXERCISE: progressively mobilize the patient as soon as it is SAFE — passive and active range of motion → sitting up → dangling/sitting at the edge of the bed → standing → transferring to a chair → ambulating — and this can begin EVEN WHILE MECHANICALLY VENTILATED in appropriately stable patients (adequate oxygenation, no escalating vasopressors/instability, secure airway, with PT/OT and adequate staffing/safety precautions). Early mobility reduces ICU-ACQUIRED WEAKNESS, delirium, ventilator and ICU days, and improves functional recovery; immobility and deep sedation, conversely, cause profound weakness and deconditioning. F — FAMILY ENGAGEMENT AND EMPOWERMENT: actively involve the family through presence/flexible visitation, participation in interdisciplinary ROUNDS and in care (reorientation, range of motion, comfort), clear communication, and SHARED DECISION-MAKING aligned with the patient's values; family engagement supports the patient (reorientation, comfort, motivation), reduces delirium, mitigates PICS for the patient and PICS-FAMILY for relatives, and is associated with better experiences and outcomes. THE BUNDLE IS COORDINATED + SYNERGISTIC: lighter sedation enables awakening/breathing trials, delirium screening, and mobility; mobility and family presence reduce delirium; daily interdisciplinary GOAL-SETTING (RN, intensivist, RT, PT/OT, pharmacist, family) ties it together — a Management-of-Care competency. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT default to continued DEEP sedation 'to keep him comfortable/quiet'; did NOT use a BENZODIAZEPINE infusion (deliriogenic); did NOT skip the daily SAT/SBT, delirium screening, or mobility; did NOT escalate sedation for agitation without first assessing/treating PAIN; did NOT keep him immobile; and did NOT use routine antipsychotics for delirium prevention. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: improving underlying illness; analgesia-first, light, non-benzodiazepine sedation targeted to RASS; a successful paired SAT/SBT enabling timely extubation; CAM-ICU monitoring with active delirium prevention; early mobility with PT/OT; an engaged proxy/family; sleep promotion + day-night cues; VAP/VTE/pressure-injury prevention woven in; and coordinated daily interdisciplinary goal-setting. EXPECTED outcome (as here): the patient is liberated from the ventilator sooner, stays free of delirium, begins mobilizing, and avoids ICU-acquired weakness and PICS — versus the deep-sedation/immobility pathway that predictably worsens all of these. NURSING ROLE: implement and coordinate the full ABCDEF bundle — pain-first, light RASS-targeted non-benzodiazepine sedation, daily SAT/SBT, CAM-ICU delirium screening and prevention, early mobility, and family engagement — with daily interdisciplinary goals, to liberate the patient and prevent the harms of prolonged critical illness.
labs.r: E: progressive EARLY MOBILITY (ROM → sit → stand → ambulate), even while ventilated when stable, with PT/OT — reduces ICU-acquired weakness/delirium/ventilator days. F: engage/empower FAMILY (presence, rounds, care participation, shared decisions) — reduces delirium + PICS/PICS-F. Bundle is synergistic + coordinated (daily interdisciplinary goals = Management of Care). Negatives: didn't default to deep sedation/benzodiazepines/immobility/routine antipsychotics; treated pain before escalating sedation. RN role: implement + coordinate the full ABCDEF bundle.

nursesNote: ICU day 3 — 60 y/o man R. Delgado, mechanically ventilated for severe CAP with hypoxemic respiratory failure (IMPROVING: FiO2/PEEP weaning, infection responding). On a continuous sedation infusion. Goal today = ICU LIBERATION via the ABCDEF bundle (not default deep sedation), to prevent oversedation, DELIRIUM, ICU-acquired weakness, prolonged ventilation, and post-intensive care syndrome (PICS). Wife (healthcare proxy) at bedside, engaged. 0700 BASELINE: DEEPLY sedated (RASS -4) — over-sedated vs a light-sedation goal; pain assessed with CPOT (can't self-report while sedated); VS HR 92, BP 126/74, vent RR 18, SpO2 96% on FiO2 0.40/PEEP 6, T 37.2. A + C (0800): treated PAIN FIRST (analgesia optimized, CPOT-guided); switched AWAY from benzodiazepine to a NON-BENZODIAZEPINE agent (dexmedetomidine/propofol) titrated to LIGHT sedation RASS 0 to -2 (calm/arousable); avoided deep sedation. B (0830): after a SAFETY SCREEN, coordinated SPONTANEOUS AWAKENING TRIAL (sedation paused/lightened) + SPONTANEOUS BREATHING TRIAL ('wake up and breathe') with RT; he awakened, followed commands, tolerated the SBT (stable vitals, adequate spontaneous breathing) → assessed ready to extubate (if failed → resume sedation at LOWEST effective dose + retry tomorrow). D + E (1000): EXTUBATED to 2 L NC after successful SAT/SBT; CAM-ICU performed (NEGATIVE); delirium PREVENTION (reorientation, glasses/hearing aid, sleep promotion/day-night cues, minimized sedatives — NO routine antipsychotics); EARLY MOBILITY begun with PT/OT (dangling/edge-of-bed, progressing); wife assisting with reorientation. F: family engaged in care + rounds + shared decisions. ICU DAY 4: light sedation no longer needed; CAM-ICU remains negative; mobility progressing (standing/ambulating with assist); sleeping at night with day-night cues + clustered care; HOB elevation + oral care (VAP prevention) + VTE/pressure-injury prophylaxis maintained; no ventilator-associated event; on track for ICU step-down. Coordinated daily interdisciplinary GOAL-SETTING (RN + intensivist + RT + PT/OT + pharmacist + family) = the management-of-care backbone. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT default to continued deep sedation 'to keep him quiet'; did NOT use a benzodiazepine infusion; did NOT skip SAT/SBT, CAM-ICU, or mobility; did NOT escalate sedation for agitation before assessing/treating pain; did NOT keep him immobile; did NOT use routine antipsychotics for delirium prevention. CONTRAST (teaching): continued DEEP sedation on a benzodiazepine without daily SAT/SBT, delirium screening, or mobility predictably → more delirium, ICU-acquired weakness, prolonged ventilation, longer stay, higher PICS risk — the harms the bundle prevents.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: ICU, mechanically ventilated for severe community-acquired pneumonia (improving); implement ICU Liberation (ABCDEF) bundle with daily interdisciplinary goals
DIAGNOSIS: mechanically ventilated patient at risk for oversedation, delirium, ICU-acquired weakness, prolonged ventilation, and post-intensive care syndrome (PICS)
CODE STATUS: full code; wife is healthcare proxy
A — ASSESS/MANAGE PAIN (first):

Assess pain routinely with a validated tool (self-report scale if able; CPOT or BPS if unable to self-report); treat pain FIRST (analgesia-first), multimodal; do not escalate sedation for agitation before assessing/treating pain
C — CHOICE OF SEDATION:
Target LIGHT sedation (RASS 0 to -2, calm/arousable) using a validated scale (RASS/SAS); titrate to the lightest effective level; avoid deep sedation unless specifically indicated
Prefer non-benzodiazepine sedatives (propofol, dexmedetomidine); AVOID benzodiazepine infusions (deliriogenic) except for specific indications (withdrawal, seizures)
B — BOTH spontaneous awakening + breathing trials (daily, paired):
Perform a daily safety screen; if passed, conduct a coordinated Spontaneous Awakening Trial (hold/lighten sedation) then Spontaneous Breathing Trial; assess readiness to extubate
Monitor continuously during trials; if a trial fails, resume sedation at the lowest effective dose and retry the next day; RN + RT coordinate
D — DELIRIUM monitoring + prevention:
Screen with a validated tool (CAM-ICU or ICDSC) at least every shift and with changes

Prevent non-pharmacologically: minimize sedatives/benzodiazepines, treat pain, reorient, provide glasses/hearing aids, promote sleep + day-night cycle (reduce noise/light, cluster care), mobilize, engage family

Do NOT use routine antipsychotics for delirium prevention; reserve for severe agitation/distress posing a safety risk (lowest dose, shortest time); identify/treat precipitants (infection, metabolic, hypoxia)
E — EARLY MOBILITY:
Progressive mobilization as safe (ROM → sitting → standing → ambulation), including while ventilated if stable; consult PT/OT; ensure safety/staffing precautions
F — FAMILY engagement:
Involve family through presence/flexible visitation, participation in rounds and care (reorientation, comfort), clear communication, and shared decision-making aligned with the patient's values
ADDITIONAL PREVENTIVE ICU CARE:

Sleep promotion + day-night cues; ventilator-associated event/pneumonia prevention (head-of-bed elevation, oral care, daily extubation-readiness assessment); VTE + pressure-injury prophylaxis; nutrition; communication method + reassurance for the awake ventilated patient
COORDINATION + DOCUMENTATION:

Daily interdisciplinary goal-setting on rounds (RN, intensivist, RT, PT/OT, pharmacist, family); document pain/sedation (CPOT/RASS) scores, SAT/SBT results, CAM-ICU, mobility level, and family involvement

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this ventilated ICU patient?
steps.inst: Select ALL considerations that are important for safe, liberation-focused ICU care.

opts.text: He is deeply sedated (RASS -4) although his pneumonia/respiratory failure is improving — deep sedation prolongs ventilation and increases delirium, so a light-sedation, liberation-focused approach is indicated
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing oversedation + the need for ICU Liberation drives the plan. RN role: reassess sedation goal + lighten.

opts.text: Assess and treat PAIN first with a validated tool (CPOT/BPS if unable to self-report), since untreated pain causes agitation often mistaken for needing more sedation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — analgesia-first prevents inappropriate sedation escalation. RN role: validated pain assessment + treat pain first.

opts.text: Target light sedation (RASS 0 to -2) with a validated scale and prefer non-benzodiazepine sedatives (propofol/dexmedetomidine), avoiding benzodiazepine infusions
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — light, non-benzodiazepine, RASS-targeted sedation reduces delirium/ventilator days. RN role: titrate to a light target + avoid benzodiazepines.

opts.text: Perform daily paired spontaneous awakening + breathing trials (after a safety screen) to assess readiness to liberate from the ventilator
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — paired SAT/SBT shortens ventilation + improves survival. RN role: safety-screen + conduct/monitor SAT+SBT.

opts.text: Screen for delirium every shift with a validated tool (CAM-ICU) and prevent it by removing precipitants (minimize sedatives, treat pain, reorient, sleep, mobilize, sensory aids, family)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — delirium is common/often hypoactive and predicts worse outcomes; prevention is mostly nonpharmacologic. RN role: CAM-ICU + precipitant removal.

opts.text: Begin early mobility (even while ventilated if stable, with PT/OT) and engage/empower the family in care and decision-making
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early mobility + family engagement reduce weakness/delirium/PICS. RN role: mobilize early + engage family.

opts.text: Coordinate the bundle with daily interdisciplinary goal-setting (RN, intensivist, RT, PT/OT, pharmacist, family)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the bundle is a coordinated, synergistic team effort. RN role: care coordination + daily goals.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Keep him deeply sedated on a continuous midazolam (benzodiazepine) infusion so he stays comfortable and 'doesn't fight the ventilator,' and avoid waking him
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Deep sedation + benzodiazepine infusions are deliriogenic and prolong ventilation/ICU stay; the evidence-based approach is analgesia-first, LIGHT non-benzodiazepine sedation with daily awakening/breathing trials — not keeping him deeply and continuously sedated.

opts.text: Keep him on strict bed rest and defer any mobility until he is fully extubated and off the unit, to avoid dislodging lines
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Early mobility (including while ventilated, when stable, with PT/OT and safety precautions) reduces ICU-acquired weakness, delirium, and ventilator days; prolonged immobility/bed rest causes profound weakness and worse outcomes.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate statements about the ABCDEF bundle.

opts.text: Untreated pain causes agitation that is often misattributed to inadequate sedation; assessing/treating pain first (with validated tools) prevents inappropriate sedation escalation
opts.rat: CORRECT — the analgesia-first (A) principle. Foundation for appropriate sedation.

opts.text: Light sedation (RASS 0 to -2) with non-benzodiazepine agents improves outcomes vs deep sedation and benzodiazepines, which prolong ventilation and increase delirium
opts.rat: CORRECT — the sedation (C) principle. Foundation for sedation choice/depth.

opts.text: Daily paired spontaneous awakening + breathing trials (after a safety screen) reduce ventilator days, ICU length of stay, and mortality; a failed trial means resuming sedation at the lowest dose and retrying
opts.rat: CORRECT — the SAT/SBT (B) principle. Foundation for liberation from the ventilator.

opts.text: Delirium is common and often hypoactive (easily missed), predicts worse outcomes, and is screened with CAM-ICU/ICDSC; prevention is mainly nonpharmacologic (remove precipitants), and routine antipsychotics are not recommended for prevention
opts.rat: CORRECT — the delirium (D) principle. Foundation for screening + prevention.

opts.text: Early mobility (even during mechanical ventilation when stable) reduces ICU-acquired weakness, delirium, and ventilator/ICU days
opts.rat: CORRECT — the early-mobility (E) principle. Foundation for preventing weakness.

opts.text: Family engagement (presence, rounds, care participation, shared decisions) supports the patient, reduces delirium, and mitigates PICS/PICS-F; the bundle elements are synergistic and coordinated by the team
opts.rat: CORRECT — the family (F) + coordination principle. Foundation for the synergistic bundle.

opts.text: Deep continuous sedation should be the default for all ventilated patients because it is the most humane and prevents the patient from remembering the ICU
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Routine deep sedation worsens outcomes (more delirium, longer ventilation/ICU stay, weakness, PICS). Light, analgesia-first, non-benzodiazepine sedation with daily awakening is the evidence-based standard.

opts.text: Routine scheduled antipsychotics should be given to every ventilated patient to prevent ICU delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Routine antipsychotics are not recommended to PREVENT (or reliably treat) delirium; prevention is nonpharmacologic (remove precipitants). Antipsychotics are reserved for dangerous agitation, briefly.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the bundle priorities for this shift

opts.text: Assess/treat PAIN first and right-size sedation to a LIGHT target (RASS 0 to -2, non-benzodiazepine), correcting the current oversedation
opts.rat: #1 — Foundational. Analgesia-first + light sedation enable every other bundle element and prevent harm. RN role: pain-first + light sedation.

opts.text: Perform the daily paired spontaneous awakening + breathing trial (after a safety screen) to assess readiness to liberate from the ventilator
opts.rat: #2 — High. Daily SAT/SBT is the key to timely extubation. RN role: SAT+SBT with RT.

opts.text: Screen for delirium (CAM-ICU) and implement nonpharmacologic prevention (reorient, sleep, sensory aids, minimize sedatives)
opts.rat: #3 — High. Delirium monitoring/prevention is core and overlaps the bundle. RN role: CAM-ICU + prevention.

opts.text: Begin/progress early mobility with PT/OT as safe
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Early mobility prevents weakness + delirium. RN role: mobilize early.

opts.text: Engage the family and coordinate the interdisciplinary daily goals/documentation
opts.rat: #5 — Important + ongoing. Family engagement + coordination tie the bundle together. RN role: family + care coordination.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for ICU Liberation.

opts.text: Assess pain with a validated tool and treat pain first before escalating sedation; target light sedation (RASS 0 to -2) with a non-benzodiazepine agent
opts.rat: Analgesia-first + light, non-benzodiazepine sedation (A + C). RN role: pain-first + light sedation.

opts.text: Perform a daily safety screen, then a coordinated spontaneous awakening trial and spontaneous breathing trial; assess extubation readiness
opts.rat: Daily paired SAT/SBT (B) liberates from the ventilator. RN role: screen + SAT+SBT.

opts.text: Screen for delirium with CAM-ICU each shift and prevent it nonpharmacologically (minimize sedatives, treat pain, reorient, sleep/day-night cues, sensory aids, mobilize, family)
opts.rat: Delirium monitoring + nonpharmacologic prevention (D). RN role: CAM-ICU + precipitant removal.

opts.text: Begin early mobility (ROM → sitting → standing → ambulation as safe), including while ventilated if stable, with PT/OT
opts.rat: Early mobility (E) prevents weakness/delirium. RN role: progressive mobilization.

opts.text: Engage the family in presence, rounds, care, and shared decision-making, and coordinate daily interdisciplinary goals
opts.rat: Family engagement (F) + coordination. RN role: family + care coordination.

opts.text: If a spontaneous breathing trial fails, resume sedation at the lowest effective dose and retry the next day
opts.rat: Correct response to a failed trial. RN role: resume minimal sedation + retry.

opts.text: Keep him deeply sedated on a continuous benzodiazepine infusion and avoid daily awakening so he 'doesn't fight the vent'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — deep sedation + benzodiazepine infusions are deliriogenic and prolong ventilation; use light, analgesia-first, non-benzodiazepine sedation with daily SAT/SBT.

opts.text: Escalate the sedation infusion for agitation without first assessing and treating pain
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — untreated pain is a common cause of agitation; assess/treat pain first (analgesia-first) before escalating sedation.

opts.text: Maintain strict bed rest and defer all mobility until after ICU discharge to protect the lines/tube
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — prolonged immobility causes ICU-acquired weakness/delirium; early mobility (with safety precautions) is indicated even during ventilation when stable.

opts.text: Start scheduled antipsychotics on every ventilated patient to prevent delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — routine antipsychotics are not recommended to prevent delirium; prevention is nonpharmacologic. Reserve antipsychotics for dangerous agitation, briefly.

opts.text: Restrict family visitation and exclude the family from rounds to reduce stimulation and let the patient rest
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — family engagement is part of the bundle (supports reorientation, reduces delirium/PICS); routinely excluding family is countertherapeutic.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess pain (validated tool) and treat it first, then right-size sedation to a light RASS target with a non-benzodiazepine agent
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Pain-first + light sedation are the foundation that enables the rest of the bundle. RN role: pain-first + light sedation.

opts.text: Complete the safety screen and perform the coordinated spontaneous awakening + breathing trial with RT; assess extubation readiness
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The daily SAT/SBT determines readiness to liberate from the ventilator. RN role: SAT+SBT.

opts.text: Screen for delirium (CAM-ICU) and implement nonpharmacologic prevention (reorient, sleep, sensory aids, minimize sedatives)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Delirium monitoring/prevention proceeds as sedation lightens and he awakens. RN role: CAM-ICU + prevention.

opts.text: Begin/progress early mobility with PT/OT as safe
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Early mobility once he is awake/stable prevents weakness. RN role: mobilize early.

opts.text: Engage the family and coordinate/document the interdisciplinary daily goals
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing. Family engagement + coordination/documentation tie the bundle together. RN role: family + coordination.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Pain was treated first and sedation lightened from RASS -4 to a target of 0 to -2 with a non-benzodiazepine agent; the patient is calm, arousable, and comfortable
opts.rat: Analgesia-first + appropriate light, non-benzodiazepine sedation. Correct, outcome-improving practice.

opts.text: A coordinated SAT/SBT was passed and the patient was successfully extubated to nasal cannula with stable gas exchange
opts.rat: Daily paired trials enabled timely, successful liberation from the ventilator. Expected favorable trajectory.

opts.text: CAM-ICU remains negative with prevention measures in place, and the patient is progressing through early mobility with PT/OT and family engagement
opts.rat: Delirium prevented + mobility progressing + family engaged. Strong recovery trajectory.

opts.text: By ICU day 4 he no longer needs sedation, is ambulating with assistance, sleeping at night, and on track for ICU step-down without delirium or significant weakness
opts.rat: Full ICU-liberation success — avoiding the harms of prolonged sedation/immobility. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the team kept him deeply sedated on a midazolam infusion without daily SAT/SBT or mobility; he developed hyperactive-then-hypoactive delirium, profound ICU-acquired weakness, and required many more ventilator days
opts.rat: ⚠️ Deep benzodiazepine sedation + no awakening/mobility caused delirium, weakness, and prolonged ventilation. Demonstrates the harms the ABCDEF bundle prevents.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — agitation was repeatedly treated by increasing the sedation infusion without assessing pain; he became more oversedated and his ventilator time lengthened
opts.rat: ⚠️ Escalating sedation for agitation without analgesia-first worsened oversedation. Demonstrates the importance of assessing/treating pain first.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — strict bed rest was maintained for days 'to protect the lines'; he developed severe ICU-acquired weakness and could not stand at discharge
opts.rat: ⚠️ Prolonged immobility caused ICU-acquired weakness. Demonstrates the value of early mobility (with safety precautions).

opts.text: Scheduled antipsychotics were started on every ventilated patient for delirium prevention, with QTc prolongation and no reduction in delirium
opts.rat: ⚠️ Routine antipsychotics do not prevent delirium and add risks (e.g., QTc). Demonstrates that prevention is nonpharmacologic (remove precipitants).

opts.text: Early in the shift, before the bundle interventions, the deeply sedated patient's status is unchanged from the prior assessment while the team sets the day's goals
opts.rat: Before the bundle is applied, the oversedated baseline is unchanged; reassessment after analgesia-first/lightened sedation + SAT/SBT will show the trajectory.

opts.text: At an ICU follow-up clinic weeks later, the patient (managed with the bundle) has minimal weakness and no significant cognitive/psychological sequelae — low PICS burden
opts.rat: The bundle's downstream benefit — reduced post-intensive care syndrome. Standard ideal long-term outcome.
